skill.name: multi-agent-analysis
skill.description: Analyze coordination models, handoff mechanisms, state sharing, and communication protocols in multi-agent systems.
skill.summary: Map how agents coordinate, transfer work, share state, and communicate across a system.
skill.author: Dowwie

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported command execution, certificate/key, weak cryptography, and reconnaissance patterns, but review found these are false positives in Markdown documentation and illustrative Python snippets. No executable scripts, real shell execution, network access, credential access, prompt injection attempts, or data exfiltration behavior were found in SKILL.md.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown and Python Examples Flagged as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The static analyzer marked Markdown code fences, ASCII diagrams, inline examples, and Python snippets as Ruby backtick execution. These sections are documentation only and do not execute commands. Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. Confidence: 0.96. Confidence reasoning: Every cited location is inside SKILL.md prose, diagram blocks, Python example blocks, or inline Markdown paths; no shell command invocation or executable script is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are Markdown documentation and example code, not executable Ruby or shell code. No command execution API, install hook, or script file exists in the analyzed file.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Agent Key Listing Flagged as Certificate Material
low_findings.description: Line 183 contains a Python example that lists agent dictionary keys for a routing prompt. It does not include certificate files, private keys, tokens, or credential material. Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. Confidence: 0.95. Confidence reasoning: The word keys appears as list(self.agents.keys()) in illustrative code, with no secret value or filesystem access.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited usage is a dictionary method in an educational routing example. There is no evidence of certificate files, private key blocks, or credential handling.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Description Text Flagged as Weak Cryptography
low_findings.description: Line 3 is the skill description about multi-agent coordination analysis. I did not find weak cryptographic algorithms or cryptographic implementation guidance in that line. Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. Confidence: 0.90. Confidence reasoning: The cited line is frontmatter text for the marketplace description and contains no crypto API or algorithm reference.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is descriptive metadata, not code. No MD5, SHA-1, DES, RC4, or comparable weak cryptographic implementation appears at the cited location.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Coordination Examples Flagged as Reconnaissance
low_findings.description: Lines 130, 133, and 256 are headings or Python examples for market bidding and hybrid coordination patterns. They do not gather host, user, process, network, or filesystem information. Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. Confidence: 0.93. Confidence reasoning: The snippets describe agent selection and state sharing concepts, with no calls to system reconnaissance commands or APIs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited text is documentation about agent bidding and hybrid state patterns. No system enumeration behavior is present.

content.value_statement: Multi-agent systems can be hard to understand because control, state, and communication are spread across agents. This skill provides a structured analysis of coordination models, handoffs, shared state, and message flow.
actual_capabilities: Classifies systems as supervisor, peer-to-peer, pipeline, or market-based coordination models.
Documents explicit, router-based, and state-based handoff mechanisms between agents.
Compares blackboard, message-passing, and hybrid state sharing patterns.
Maps direct invocation, queue-based, and event-driven communication protocols.
Produces a reusable Markdown analysis template for multi-agent architecture reviews.
limitations: Does not execute code or automatically inspect repositories without model or tool support.
Does not verify runtime behavior, latency, or failure rates from production telemetry.
Does not provide a formal security review of each agent implementation.
Works best when the user provides code paths, diagrams, or architecture notes.

use_cases.title: Review Agent Framework Architecture
use_cases.description: Identify how a framework routes tasks, preserves context, and prevents handoff loops.

use_cases.title: Compare Orchestration Designs
use_cases.description: Create consistent notes for comparing supervisor, pipeline, peer, and market-based systems.

use_cases.title: Document Existing Agent Systems
use_cases.description: Turn scattered implementation details into a clear map of agents, state, and communication paths.

prompt_templates.title: Identify the Coordination Model
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of a known architecture.
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this multi-agent system and identify its coordination model. Explain whether it uses a supervisor, peer-to-peer, pipeline, or market-based pattern.

prompt_templates.title: Map Handoffs and State
prompt_templates.prompt: Review these agent files and document how control is handed off. Include what context is preserved and how state is shared or isolated.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Communication Protocols
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare the communication protocols in these multi-agent implementations. Classify direct calls, queues, events, and any asynchronous behavior.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Coordination Risks
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced analysis before redesign or production release.
prompt_templates.prompt: Perform a full multi-agent coordination analysis. Include agent inventory, control flow, handoff boundaries, shared state risks, loop prevention, and design tradeoffs.

output_examples.input: Analyze a framework with one router agent and three worker agents.
output_examples.output: The system uses a supervisor model. The router controls task assignment, workers do not delegate, and shared context is preserved through the router.

output_examples.input: Review agents that communicate through task and result queues.
output_examples.output: The communication model is queue-based and asynchronous. Coupling is low, but debugging requires tracing queue producers, consumers, and message identifiers.

output_examples.input: Compare a blackboard design with message passing for agent state.
output_examples.output: The blackboard design improves visibility but increases coupling and race risk. Message passing improves isolation but needs stronger retry and ordering rules.

best_practices: Provide the relevant orchestrator, agent, queue, and state-management files before asking for analysis.
Ask for evidence-backed locations when using the skill on a real codebase.
Use the output template consistently when comparing multiple frameworks or versions.
anti_patterns: Do not treat illustrative snippets as confirmed runtime behavior without repository evidence.
Do not ignore loop prevention and context preservation when reviewing handoff designs.
Do not compare frameworks without using the same criteria for each system.

faq.question: What does this skill analyze?
faq.answer: It analyzes coordination models, handoff mechanisms, state sharing, communication protocols, and loop prevention in multi-agent systems.

faq.question: Does it run code?
faq.answer: No. The skill is documentation and analysis guidance. Any code inspection depends on the host AI tool and user-provided context.

faq.question: Can it compare different agent frameworks?
faq.answer: Yes. It provides common categories that make framework comparisons more consistent and easier to review.

faq.question: What input works best?
faq.answer: Architecture notes, orchestrator files, agent definitions, state stores, queue handlers, and communication diagrams work best.

faq.question: Does it replace a security audit?
faq.answer: No. It can identify coordination risks, but it does not perform a full security review of implementation code.

faq.question: Which tools can use it?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
